Metric: Law on childhood gender-affirming care
Source Name: AWMF S2k Guideline on Gender Incongruence/Gender Dysphoria in Childhood and Adolescence
Notes: Guideline approved in 2025 for Germany, Austria, and Switzerland; provides clinical recommendations but is not a statute.

Notes: German studies discuss puberty blockers and hormonal-treatment initiation trends, but a clean national minors-only blocker prescription rate was not identified in the accessible source set.

Metric: Cross-sex hormone therapy initiation in minors
Notes: A German claims-data study examined hormonal-treatment initiation between 2010 and 2021, but the public abstract snippet did not provide a minors-only national initiation percentage.

Metric: Youth with gender dysphoria/incongruence receiving medical interventions
Breakdown: Individuals aged 4-30 with prevalent GD receiving hormonal therapy
Source Name: Gottschalk et al., European Journal of Health Economics
Notes: This is the closest published Germany-specific figure located: 58% of prevalent GD cases aged 4-30 received hormonal therapies in 2019. It is not limited to minors only.

Metric: Diagnostic stability of gender dysphoria in youth
Breakdown: Confirmed F64 diagnosis retained after 5 years
Source Name: Bachmann et al., Dtsch Arztebl Int / PMC
Notes: In the longitudinal cohort, only 36.4% still had a confirmed F64 diagnosis after five years.

Metric: Diagnostic stability of gender dysphoria in youth
Breakdown: No confirmed F64 diagnosis after 5 years
Source Name: Bachmann et al., Dtsch Arztebl Int / PMC
Notes: This is diagnostic non-persistence, not the same thing as formal detransition.

Metric: Detransition or desistance rates
Source Name: Bachmann et al. / German claims-data literature
Notes:
Most concrete German data (2024 insurance study, Nettermann et al., cited in TRANSKIDS-CARE and guideline context):

5-year persistence of a confirmed gender-identity diagnosis (F64) = only 36.4%.
Therefore desistance rate = 63.6% overall (ages 5–24).
15–19-year-old females: lowest persistence = 27.3% → desistance 72.7%.
20–24-year-old males: highest persistence = 49.7% → desistance 50.3%.
Guideline notes: Desistance is common pre-puberty; after starting medical transition in adolescence it is rare (0–4% in follow-up studies) if guideline-compliant.

Metric: Regret rates after gender-affirming interventions in minors
Source Name: Nieder et al. / ISSM summary of Hamburg GIS cohort
Notes: Out of 1,359 individuals who began hormonal treatment, 7.9% discontinued their established hormonal GR (gender reassignment) during an average follow-up of 8.5 years.

Metric: Age distribution of first medical intervention
Notes: German studies discuss treatment initiation trends and age groups, but a clean national age distribution of first medical intervention was not identified in the accessible source set.
